perturbation.original_text: Sabco shall provide at its expense a staff member to handle race team's media and public relations needs and shall cause Prolong to receive prominent mention and display within press kits featuring the Sabco No. 40, 42 and 46 race cars.
perturbation.changed_text: Sabco shall endeavor to provide a staff member to handle race team's media and public relations needs and will aim for Prolong to receive mention within press kits featuring the Sabco No. 40, 42 and 46 race cars.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall provide' to 'shall endeavor to provide' weakens the obligation to provide a staff member. Also, changing 'shall cause Prolong to receive prominent mention and display' to 'will aim for Prolong to receive mention' makes the obligation for prominent mention and display non-mandatory, introducing ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: North Carolina General Statute § 75-1.1: Unfair methods of competition, unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of commerce unlawful
perturbation.law_explanation: By weakening the guarantee of a dedicated staff member and prominent placement in press kits, Sabco could be interpreted as engaging in a deceptive practice if Prolong reasonably relied on those provisions for marketing and business advantages. While a direct violation is not explicit, the ambiguity creates a scenario where Sabco's actions could be construed as unfair or deceptive if the level of support provided is significantly less than reasonably expected, potentially misleading Prolong about the value of the sponsorship, violating the North Carolina statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ G.S. 75-1.1 § 75-1.1. Methods of competition, acts and practices regulated; legislative policy. (a) Unfair methods of competition in or affecting commerce, and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce, are declared unlawful. (b) For purposes of this section, "commerce" includes all business activities, however denominated, but does not include professional services rendered by a member of a learned profession. (c) Nothing in this section shall apply to acts done by the publisher, owner, agent, or employee of a newspaper, periodical or radio or television station, or other advertising medium in the publication or dissemination of an advertisement, when the owner, agent or employee did not have knowledge of the false, misleading or deceptive character of the advertisement and when the newspaper, periodical or radio or television station, or other advertising medium did not have a direct financial interest in the sale or distribution of the advertised product or service. (d) Any party claiming to be exempt from the provisions of this section shall have the burden of proof with respect to such claim. (1969, c. 833; 1977, c. 747, ss. 1, 2.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding Sabco's obligations to provide a staff member and ensure Prolong's prominent mention, potentially creating a deceptive practice. The original contract stated Sabco 'shall provide' and 'shall cause,' creating a clear obligation. The changed text weakens these obligations by using 'shall endeavor to provide' and 'will aim for,' making the obligations less mandatory. This ambiguity could be viewed as an unfair or deceptive practice under North Carolina G.S. 75-1.1, as it impacts commerce and potentially misleads Prolong about the value of the sponsorship. The snippet states 'Unfair methods of competition in or affecting commerce, and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce, are declared unlawful.' This supports the possibility of a legal contradiction because the altered text creates an ambiguous scenario where Sabco's level of support is not guaranteed, potentially misleading Prolong.

perturbation.original_text: Sabco shall indemnify, defend and hold harmless Prolong, its officers, directors, employees and representatives from and against any and all losses, claims, suits, damages, liabilities, costs and expenses, including attorney fees and count costs incurred by any of them arising out of: (i) Any breach of any warrant made by Sabco herein; (ii) Any acts done or words spoken (iii) Any claims by any persons arising from acts or omissions of any nature by Sabco, its employees or agents, including but not limited to, claims arising during the court of competition or practice in the performance of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Sabco will endeavor to indemnify, defend and hold harmless Prolong, its officers, directors, employees and representatives from losses, claims, suits, damages, liabilities, costs and expenses, including attorney fees and count costs reasonably related to: (i) A breach of warranties by Sabco; (ii) Certain acts or words; (iii) Some claims arising from acts or omissions by Sabco, its employees or agents during competition or practice related to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall indemnify' to 'will endeavor to indemnify' significantly weakens the indemnification obligation. The addition of 'reasonably related to' and 'certain acts or words' introduces ambiguity about the scope of indemnification. Limiting the claims to 'some claims' creates further uncertainty. This creates an ambiguous obligation that contradicts North Carolina law regarding contractual obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: North Carolina General Statute § 22B-1: Agreements, covenants, promises or understandings relieving from negligence in construction, repair, or maintenance of real property unenforceable
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly related to real property, the weakening of the indemnification clause could, in a hypothetical scenario, attempt to relieve Sabco from liability for its own negligence (through 'certain acts or words' or 'some claims' clauses) in a way that is so ambiguous that would not hold up under scrutiny in North Carolina law regarding contractual obligations, specifically related to the spirit of agreements relieving negligence.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ G.S. 22B-1 Chapter 22B. Contracts Against Public Policy. Article 1. Invalid Agreements. § 22B-1. Certain indemnity and defend agreements invalid. (a) Provisions in, or in connection with, a construction agreement or design professional agreement purporting to require a promisor to indemnify or hold harmless the promisee, the promisee's independent contractors, agents, employees, or indemnitees against liability for damages arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property proximately caused by or resulting from the negligence, in whole or in part, of the promisee, its independent contractors, agents, employees, or indemnitees, is against public policy, void and unenforceable. Nothing contained in this subsection shall prevent or prohibit a contract, promise or agreement whereby a promisor shall indemnify or hold harmless any promisee or the promisee's independent contractors, agents, employees or indemnitees against liability for damages resulting from the sole negligence of the promisor, its agents or employees. (b) Provisions in, or in connection with, a construction agreement or design professional agreement purporting to require a promisor to indemnify or hold harmless the promisee, the promisee's independent contractors, agents, employees, indemnitees, or any other person or entity against losses, damages, or expenses are against public policy, void, and unenforceable unless the the fault of the promisor or its derivative parties is a proximate cause of the loss, damage, or expense indemnified. (c) Provisions in, or in connection with, a construction agreement that includes design professional services or a design professional agreement purporting to require a design professional to defend a promisee, the promisee's independent contractors, agents, or employees, the promisee's indemnitees, or any other person or entity against liability or claims for damages or expenses, including attorney's fees, proximately caused or allegedly caused by the professional neg
perturbation.justification: The original text states that Sabco 'shall indemnify', creating a clear obligation. The changed text weakens this obligation by stating Sabco 'will endeavor to indemnify', which creates ambiguity. The 'Perturbation Explanation' and 'Law Explanation' both highlight this weakening. The addition of 'reasonably related to', 'certain acts or words', and 'some claims' introduces further ambiguity. Scraped Law Snippet 1, G.S. 22B-1, addresses indemnity agreements, and although it focuses on construction and design agreements, the underlying principle that indemnity agreements should be clear and enforceable is relevant. The modified language undermines this principle by creating an ambiguous obligation, which contradicts the spirit of clear contractual obligations. The change introduces an ambiguity that weakens the indemnification obligation enough to create a potential legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Prolong shall use its sponsorship of the Sabco entries for advertising, promoting and marketing itself.
perturbation.changed_text: Prolong should consider using its sponsorship of the Sabco entries for advertising, promoting and marketing itself.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall use' to 'should consider using', the obligation for Prolong to use the sponsorship for advertising, promoting, and marketing is made non-mandatory. This creates an ambiguous legal obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: North Carolina General Statute § 75-1.1: Unfair methods of competition, unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of commerce unlawful
perturbation.law_explanation: If Sabco represented that Prolong *must* use the sponsorship for advertising to ensure the sponsorship benefits both parties, but the contract now states Prolong 'should consider', it creates a situation where Sabco could be argued to have engaged in a deceptive practice. If Prolong's lack of advertising resulted in failure of the partnership. Sabco might be in trouble.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ G.S. 75-1.1 § 75-1.1. Methods of competition, acts and practices regulated; legislative policy. (a) Unfair methods of competition in or affecting commerce, and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce, are declared unlawful. (b) For purposes of this section, "commerce" includes all business activities, however denominated, but does not include professional services rendered by a member of a learned profession. (c) Nothing in this section shall apply to acts done by the publisher, owner, agent, or employee of a newspaper, periodical or radio or television station, or other advertising medium in the publication or dissemination of an advertisement, when the owner, agent or employee did not have knowledge of the false, misleading or deceptive character of the advertisement and when the newspaper, periodical or radio or television station, or other advertising medium did not have a direct financial interest in the sale or distribution of the advertised product or service. (d) Any party claiming to be exempt from the provisions of this section shall have the burden of proof with respect to such claim. (1969, c. 833; 1977, c. 747, ss. 1, 2.)
perturbation.justification: The change from "shall use" to "should consider using" weakens the obligation. The Law Explanation suggests that if the original intent was for Prolong to *must* use the sponsorship for advertising, and this is weakened, it opens up Sabco to claims of deceptive practices under G.S. 75-1.1 (a), which declares unlawful "unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce."
